canonical.summary: Small dark brown to black lesions first develop on older/lower foliage that expand to about 1/4-1/2 inch with characteristic concentric rings producing a 'bull's eye' or 'target-spot' appearance; surrounding tissue becomes yellow and entire leaves may turn chlorotic. Stem infections form oval to irregular dry brown areas with dark brown concentric rings, and fruit lesions are sunken, leathery, and dark brown to black with raised concentric ridges near the stem, often covered with a black mass of spores.
diagnostic_features: Characteristic 'bull's-eye' or 'target-spot' concentric rings in darkened lesion areas on leaves, dark brown concentric rings on stem lesions, raised concentric ridges on leathery black fruit lesions near the stem, and presence of long-beaked Alternaria spores (12-20 x 120-296 um with 9-11 transverse septa).
treatments: Use resistant or tolerant cultivars (e.g., 'Mountain Magic', 'Mountain Fresh Plus F1', 'Bush Celebrity Hybrid', 'Big Beef Hybrid', 'Celebrity Hybrid', 'Rutgers', 'Juliet F1', 'Tommy Toe', 'Old Brooks', 'Cabernet F1', or varieties designated 'EB')
Start with disease-free/pathogen-free seed, transplants, and seed tubers; collect seed only from disease-free plants
Rotate out of tomatoes and related crops for 2-3 years to non-susceptible crops
Control susceptible weeds (e.g., horsenettle, black nightshade, hairy nightshade) and eliminate volunteer tomato plants and cull piles
Plow under or remove and destroy crop residue/infected plants at end of season to reduce overwintering
Maintain plant vigor with proper fertilization (adequate N and P, avoid over-fertilization with potassium)
Stake or trellis plants to improve air circulation and reduce soil contact
Space plants adequately apart and orient rows with prevailing winds; avoid shaded areas and wind barriers
Apply plastic or organic mulch (fabric, straw, dried leaves) to provide barrier between contaminated soil and leaves
Prune bottom leaves (but no more than one-third) to prevent spore splash and increase airflow
Use drip irrigation or water at base of plants instead of overhead/sprinkler; irrigate early in day to promote rapid drying
Avoid working with plants when wet from rain, irrigation, or dew
Wash hands and sanitize pruning tools after contact with infected plants
Remove leaves with leaf spots and bury or burn them
For greenhouse production, cover houses with UV-absorbing vinyl film (reduces early blight by up to 50%)
Scout regularly beginning in mid to late June and apply fungicides preventatively, timed when environmental conditions favor disease (e.g., using TOM-CAST spore trapping/forecasting)

Apply conventional fungicides on a 7-14 day interval, alternating between chemical families to prevent resistance (rotating away from FRAC group 11/strobilurins); products include azoxystrobin + chlorothalonil (Quadris Opti), azoxystrobin + difenoconazole (Quadris Top), boscalid (Endura), chlorothalonil (Bravo), cyprodinil + fludioxinil (Switch), difenoconazole + benzovindiflupyr (Aprovia Top), difenoconazole + cyprodinil (Inspire Super), fenamidone (Reason), flutriafol (Rhyme), fluopyram + trifloxystrobin (Luna Sensation), fluopyram + pyrimethanil (Luna Tranquility), fluxapyroxad + pyraclostrobin (Priaxor), mandipropamid + difenoconazole (Revus Top), penthiopyrad (Fontelis), pydiflumetofen + fludioxonil (Miravis Prime), pyrimethanil (Scala), strobilurins (Quadris, Cabrio, Gem, Evito)

Organic options: Bacillus amyloliquefaciens strain D747 (Double Nickel 55), Bacillus mycoides isolate J (LifeGard WG), Bacillus pumilus strain QST 2808 (Sonata), Bacillus subtilis strain IAB/BS03 (AVIV), Bacillus subtilis strain QST 713 (Cease, Serenade ASO, Serenade Opti), extract of Reynoutria sachalinensis (Regalia), fixed copper, polyoxin D zinc salt (OSO 5%SC), potassium bicarbonate (Carb-O-Nator), Streptomyces lydicus WYEC 108 (Actinovate AG)

summary.quote: Initially, small dark spots form on older foliage near the ground. Leaf spots are round, brown and can grow up to 1/2 inch in diameter. Larger spots have target-like concentric rings. The tissue around spots often turns yellow. ... Stem infections on older plants are oval to irregular, dry brown areas with dark brown concentric rings. ... Fruit spots are leathery and black, with raised concentric ridges. They generally occur near the stem.

diagnostic_features.quote: Tomato leaves showing characteristic "bull's-eye" leaf spots appear dry and papery with concentric rings... Presence of typical symptoms and long-beaked Alternaria spores in the above described size range are diagnostic for this diseases.

treatments.quote: Early blight-resistant varieties are readily available. ... Cover the soil under the plants with mulch, such as fabric, straw, plastic mulch, or dried leaves. Water at the base of each plant, using drip irrigation, a soaker hose, or careful hand watering. Increase airflow by staking or trellising, removing weeds, and spacing plants adequately apart Pruning the bottom leaves can also prevent early blight spores from splashing up from the soil onto leaves. Let two years pass before you plant tomatoes or peppers in the same location. ... It is important to alternate between different chemical families to avoid the development of pathogen insensitivity to particular active ingredients.

affected_parts.quote: It affects leaves, fruits and stems and can be severely yield-limiting when susceptible tomato cultivars are used and weather is favorable. ... The pathogen also survives on tomato seed or may be introduced on tomato transplants.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Early blight of tomato is caused primarily by the fungus Alternaria linariae (= A. tomatophila ; formerly known as A. solani)

type_of_disease.quote: Early blight of tomato is caused primarily by the fungus Alternaria linariae

canonical.summary: A brown to black lesion develops on the stem near the soil line, girdling the stem and causing sudden permanent wilt of above-ground parts; under moist conditions white mycelium and tan to reddish-brown spherical sclerotia (1–2 mm) develop on the lesion, and fruit in contact with infested soil develop sunken, water-soaked, star-shaped spots.
diagnostic_features: Thick mats of white mycelium radiating from the plant onto the soil surface with tan to reddish-brown to black spherical sclerotia (about 1–2 mm, mustard-seed sized) on the mycelial mat at the soil line; star-shaped, water-soaked lesions on fruit; no offensive odor associated with rotted fruit, at least initially.
treatments: Avoid planting in fields with a history of southern blight
Rotate host crops with corn, wheat, barley, or other non-host crops to reduce inoculum levels in soil
Modify planting dates to avoid conditions that favor disease development, when possible
Bury infected plant debris and sclerotia via deep plowing to invert the soil
Ensure the previous crop is decomposed prior to planting (disk the field several times in fall and spring)
Remove symptomatic plants and reduce or eliminate weed populations
Remove all plants (including roots) and three inches of soil from at least 12 inches beyond the infested area; bag and dispose in a landfill
Grow non-susceptible plants (e.g., larger woody ornamentals) in the affected area for two to three years to allow time for sclerotia to die naturally
Maintain adequate soil pH for optimum plant growth (lower soil pH encourages disease)
Use grafted tomato rootstocks such as 'Maxifort', 'Big Power', and 'Beaufort'
Inspect new plants for sclerotia and mycelia of A. rolfsii prior to transplanting
Use high-quality mulches (e.g., shredded oak bark, red cedar) and avoid mulches that may be contaminated
Thin existing gardens or space plants farther apart to improve airflow and promote rapid drying of foliage and soil
Fumigate soils with broad-spectrum chemicals such as chloropicrin and sodium metam sodium
Fungicides: difenoconazole + benzovindiflupyr (Aprovia Top)
Fungicides: fluoxastrobin (Aftershock, Evito 480 SC)
Fungicides: fluxapyroxad + pyraclostrobin (Priaxor 500 SC)
Fungicides: penthiopyrad (Fontelis)
Fungicides: pyraclostrobin (Cabrio EG)
Fungicides containing azoles (e.g., propiconazole, tebuconazole), fludioxonil, flutolanil, mancozeb, PCNB, strobilurins, thiophanate-methyl, and triadimefon
Alternate use of two or more unrelated active ingredients to minimize fungicide-resistant strains

summary.value: A brown to black lesion develops on the stem near the soil line, girdling the stem and causing sudden permanent wilt of above-ground parts; under moist conditions white mycelium and tan to reddish-brown spherical sclerotia (1–2 mm) develop on the lesion, and fruit in contact with infested soil develop sunken, water-soaked, star-shaped spots.
summary.quote: Initially, a brown to black lesion usually develops on the stem near the soil line. The lesion will develop rapidly and can completely girdle the stem, which will cause a sudden and permanent wilt of all above ground parts. ... Under moist conditions, white mycelium will typically develop on stem lesions ... After a few days, tan to reddish-brown, spherical sclerotia (1 to 2 mm in diameter) can appear on the mat of mycelia. ... Lesions will initially appear as sunken and slightly yellow areas that later become water-soaked, soft, and star-shaped spots.

diagnostic_features.value: Thick mats of white mycelium radiating from the plant onto the soil surface with tan to reddish-brown to black spherical sclerotia (about 1–2 mm, mustard-seed sized) on the mycelial mat at the soil line; star-shaped, water-soaked lesions on fruit; no offensive odor associated with rotted fruit, at least initially.
diagnostic_features.quote: Thick mats of white fungal threads (called mycelia) may grow from infected tissue, radiating from the plant onto the soil surface.  Sclerotia (small spherical structures that are about the size of mustard seeds) develop on infected tissue and on the soil surface.  Sclerotia range in color from light tan to dark reddish-brown to black.

look_alikes.quote: There have been a few reports of Southern blight ( Sclerotinia rolfsii ) and White mold ( Sclerotinia sclerotiorum ) on tomato and pepper in New Jersey.

treatments.quote: Avoid planting in fields with a history of southern blight. Rotate host crops with corn, wheat, barley, or other non-host crops to reduce inoculum levels in soil. Modify planting dates to avoid conditions that favor disease development, when possible. Inoculum levels can be reduced by burying infected plant debris and sclerotia via deep plowing to invert the soil. ... Tomato varieties grafted onto tomato rootstocks 'Maxifort', 'Big Power', and 'Beaufort' have been shown to maintain high yields in southern-blight infested fields in one study. ... Several fungicides are labeled for use on tomato and pepper to manage southern blight ... The fumigation of soils with broad spectrum chemicals such as chloropicrin and sodium metam sodium can reduce disease incidence

affected_parts.value: Stem; Whole plant; Foliar; Root; Fruit
affected_parts.quote: Southern blight initially leads to a water-soaked appearance on lower leaves or water-soaked lesions (spots) on lower stems.  Any plant part that is near or in contact with the soil may become infected.  Infected plants yellow and wilt... Fruit rots, crown rots and root rots are also typical symptoms of the disease.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern blight is caused by the fungus  Athelia rolfsii  (formerly  Sclerotium rolfsii )

type_of_disease.quote: Southern blight is caused by the soil-born fungus Agroathelia rolfsii

canonical.summary: Entire plants may become yellow and wilt with leaves turning brown and remaining attached, with a dark brown girdling lesion at the soil surface accompanied by white fanlike mats of fungal mycelium and small round sclerotia on the lower stem.
diagnostic_features: Conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium on the base of the stem and soil surface, with numerous small round sclerotia about the size of mustard seeds that progress from yellow-tan to reddish-brown to dark brown at maturity.
treatments: Alternating soybean or other susceptible crops with nonhost crops such as maize, grain sorghum, wheat, or pasture grasses or clean fallow for 2 years may prevent the buildup of inoculum to damaging levels.

summary.value: Entire plants may become yellow and wilt with leaves turning brown and remaining attached, with a dark brown girdling lesion at the soil surface accompanied by white fanlike mats of fungal mycelium and small round sclerotia on the lower stem.
summary.quote: Later in the season, entire plants may become yellow and wilt, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached to the plant. A dark brown lesion that girdles the stem occurs at the soil surface. This lesion is generally accompanied by the development of conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium that form on the base of the stem, on leaf residue, and on the soil surface around infected plants.

diagnostic_features.value: Conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium on the base of the stem and soil surface, with numerous small round sclerotia about the size of mustard seeds that progress from yellow-tan to reddish-brown to dark brown at maturity.
diagnostic_features.quote: This lesion is generally accompanied by the development of conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium that form on the base of the stem, on leaf residue, and on the soil surface around infected plants. Numerous, small round fungal bodies that are about the size of mustard seeds (called sclerotia) form on these fungal mats and on the lower stem. Initially, sclerotia are yellow-tan then progress to a reddish-brown color and finally dark brown at maturity.

treatments.value: Alternating soybean or other susceptible crops with nonhost crops such as maize, grain sorghum, wheat, or pasture grasses or clean fallow for 2 years may prevent the buildup of inoculum to damaging levels.

affected_parts.quote: Seedling infection results in pre- or post-emergence damping-off. Later in the season, entire plants may become yellow and wilt, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached to the plant. A dark brown lesion that girdles the stem occurs at the soil surface.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern blight is caused by Agroathelia rolfsii , a soilborne fungus that has a wide host range of more than 200 plant species.

type_of_disease.quote: Southern blight is caused by Agroathelia rolfsii , a soilborne fungus that has a wide host range of more than 200 plant species.

canonical.summary: Light purple spots and areas develop on the top surface of leaves exposed to light, deepening to reddish-purple or bronze and giving leaves a leathery, 'sunburned' appearance, with reddish-purple lesions also on petioles, stems, and pods; symptoms typically appear on uppermost leaves during late reproductive stages (R5-R6).
diagnostic_features: Purplish or bronzed discoloration on the uppermost, sun-exposed leaves giving a leathery, shiny 'sunburned' appearance, typically during late reproductive stages (R5-R6), with reddish-purple lesions on petioles, stems, and pods.
treatments: Use pathogen-free seed and plant partially resistant varieties with low susceptibility
Crop rotation with nonhost crops (such as corn or small grains)
Tillage to break down soybean residue and reduce inoculum
Foliar fungicide applications, particularly in fields with disease history
Seed treatment fungicides
Regular scouting during reproductive stages for early detection

summary.value: Light purple spots and areas develop on the top surface of leaves exposed to light, deepening to reddish-purple or bronze and giving leaves a leathery, 'sunburned' appearance, with reddish-purple lesions also on petioles, stems, and pods; symptoms typically appear on uppermost leaves during late reproductive stages (R5-R6).
summary.quote: The first symptom of Cercospora leaf blight is the development of light purple spots and areas on the top surface of leaves exposed to light. The discolored areas expand and deepen in color to a reddish-purple or bronze. The infected leaves appear leathery and 'sunburned'.

diagnostic_features.value: Purplish or bronzed discoloration on the uppermost, sun-exposed leaves giving a leathery, shiny 'sunburned' appearance, typically during late reproductive stages (R5-R6), with reddish-purple lesions on petioles, stems, and pods.
diagnostic_features.quote: The most distinctive symptom of CLB is the purplish or bronzed discoloration that appears on the uppermost leaves of infected plants, typically during the late reproductive stages (R5-R6). This discoloration gives the affected leaves a leathery, shiny appearance.

treatments.value: Use pathogen-free seed and plant partially resistant varieties with low susceptibility; Crop rotation with nonhost crops (such as corn or small grains); Tillage to break down soybean residue and reduce inoculum; Foliar fungicide applications, particularly in fields with disease history; Seed treatment fungicides; Regular scouting during reproductive stages for early detection
treatments.quote: Resistant Varieties: While there are no soybean varieties fully resistant to C. kikuchii , some varieties show partial resistance to CLB and purple seed stain. Planting these varieties can help reduce the severity of the disease. Crop Rotation and Residue Management: Since C. kikuchii survives in soybean residue, crop rotation with nonhost crops (such as corn or small grains) can help reduce inoculum levels in the field. Additionally, practices like tillage can help break down soybean residue, thereby reducing the amount of inoculum available to infect the next soybean crop. Fungicide Applications: Fungicides can be an effective tool for managing CLB, particularly in fields with a history of the disease... Scouting and Field Monitoring: Regular scouting is critical for early detection of CLB... Seed Quality Management: seed treatments may be necessary to protect seed lots intended for planting.

affected_parts.quote: Reddish-purple lesions may also occur on petioles, stems, and pods.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cercospora leaf blight is caused by the fungus  Cercospora kikuchii .

type_of_disease.quote: Cercospora leaf blight is caused by the fungus  Cercospora kikuchii .

canonical.summary: Leaf lesions are small, irregular to circular in shape, and gray with reddish-brown borders, occurring most commonly on the upper leaf surface and starting as dark, water-soaked spots; severe cases lead to premature leaf drop and can spread to stems and pods.
diagnostic_features: As lesions age, the central area becomes gray to light brown with dark, red-brown margins; seed stain caused by C. sojina is light purple to gray (versus royal purple from CLB pathogens).
treatments: Plant resistant soybean varieties (varieties with resistance to all known races are available)
Rotate to a non-host crop and use tillage to reduce survival of C. sojina (alfalfa, corn, and small grains are not susceptible)
Apply foliar fungicides during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages

summary.quote: Leaf lesions are small, irregular to circular in shape, and gray with reddish-brown borders. Most commonly occurring on the upper leaf surface, lesions start as dark, water-soaked spots that vary in size.

diagnostic_features.value: As lesions age, the central area becomes gray to light brown with dark, red-brown margins; seed stain caused by C. sojina is light purple to gray (versus royal purple from CLB pathogens).
diagnostic_features.quote: As lesions age, the central area becomes gray to light brown with dark, red-brown margins.

look_alikes.quote: The foliar phase of FLS is usually severe and easily distinguishable from CLB when FLS petiole lesions occur.

treatments.value: Plant resistant soybean varieties (varieties with resistance to all known races are available); Rotate to a non-host crop and use tillage to reduce survival of C. sojina (alfalfa, corn, and small grains are not susceptible); Apply foliar fungicides during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages
treatments.quote: Resistant soybean varieties are available and should be used where disease is a potential problem. Several races of the pathogen have been identified, and varieties with resistance to all known races are available. Rotating to a non-host crop and tillage will reduce survival of C. sojina . Crops not susceptible to this pathogen are alfalfa, corn, and small grains. Foliar fungicides applied during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages can reduce frogeye leaf spot.

affected_parts.quote: Severe cases lead to premature leaf drop and can spread to stems and pods. The fungus that causes frogeye leaf spot may also cause purple petiole lesions (Figure 9) and infect seed.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Frogeye leaf spot is caused by the fungus Cercospora sojina .

type_of_disease.quote: Frogeye leaf spot is caused by the fungus Cercospora sojina .

profiles.disease: Bacterial Canker And Wilt Of Tomato

canonical.summary: Symptoms include small white raised spots on leaves, leaf edge browning with a yellow border, wilting (often one-sided), brown streaks in the vascular system, stem splits forming long brown cankers, and bird's-eye spots (small raised white spots with tan or brown centers) on fruit; the bacterium can become systemic, causing whole-plant wilt and collapse.
diagnostic_features: Bird's-eye spots on fruit (small ~1/4 inch raised white spots with tan or brown centers); brown streaks visible in the vascular system when the stem is cut open; yellow sticky fluid may emerge from a cut stem when squeezed; lower leaves wilt, often only on one side.
look_alikes: Other tomato diseases that occur in Minnesota
treatments: Plant tomatoes where no tomatoes, potatoes, peppers or eggplants have been for the past 3-4 years
Purchase/plant certified pathogen-free seed from a reputable supplier; do not save seeds from infected plants
Treat seed with dilute sodium hypochlorite or via hot water treatment (e.g., soak in 122°F water for 25 minutes)
Soak seeds in a solution of 1 part germicidal bleach to 4 parts water for 1 minute, then rinse in water for 5 minutes
Inspect transplants and choose plants without spots or discoloration; avoid transplants that have been pruned or cut back
Keep tomato leaves as dry as possible: water with drip irrigation or a soaker hose, water in the morning, use filtered/treated irrigation water
Space plants for good air flow; stake or cage plants and clean/sanitize stakes and cages each year
Disinfect tools and equipment with a commercial greenhouse sanitizer or 1:9 bleach:water solution; discard strings and wooden stakes from infested fields
Wash hands, exposed clothing and boots regularly; designate boots and clothes for each greenhouse or bay
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet with rain, irrigation or dew
Scout regularly; isolate symptomatic plants and submit samples for diagnosis
Promptly remove and destroy infected plants (and adjacent plants in greenhouses/high tunnels); bag and remove including roots
Diseased plants can be buried where tomatoes and peppers will not be grown for 3 years, or burned
Remove or plow in remaining plant debris immediately after harvest to encourage decomposition
Crop rotation away from tomato and related Solanaceae crops for 2-4 years
Control weeds in the Solanaceae family (e.g., nightshade) and remove volunteer tomatoes/peppers
Use new potting media stored in a sanitary environment; wash and sanitize re-used pots and trays
Regular preventative sprays of copper compounds (most effective during transplant production rather than after symptoms appear in the field)

summary.value: Symptoms include small white raised spots on leaves, leaf edge browning with a yellow border, wilting (often one-sided), brown streaks in the vascular system, stem splits forming long brown cankers, and bird's-eye spots (small raised white spots with tan or brown centers) on fruit; the bacterium can become systemic, causing whole-plant wilt and collapse.
summary.quote: Small, white, raised spots may form on leaves. Leaf edge discoloration and wilting... Yellow to tan patches form between veins. Edges of leaf turn brown, with a yellow border... Brown streaks can be seen in the vascular system when the stem is cut open. Stem splits, forming long, brown cankers... Fruit may have small (1/4 inch), raised, white spots with tan or brown centers (called bird's eye spot).

diagnostic_features.value: Bird's-eye spots on fruit (small ~1/4 inch raised white spots with tan or brown centers); brown streaks visible in the vascular system when the stem is cut open; yellow sticky fluid may emerge from a cut stem when squeezed; lower leaves wilt, often only on one side.
diagnostic_features.quote: Fruit may have small (1/4 inch), raised, white spots with tan or brown centers (called bird's eye spot)... Brown streaks can be seen in the vascular system when the stem is cut open... Yellow sticky fluid may emerge from cut stem when squeezed... Lower leaves wilt, often only on one side.

canonical.summary: Green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy, with possible puckering and distortion of leaves and stunting of plants; pods may be misshapen with mottling and reduced size, and seeds may appear smoky or have seed coat mottling.
diagnostic_features: Mild chlorotic mottling to severe mosaic on younger leaves in the upper canopy, green stem after pod maturity, leaf distortion and puckering; symptom development favored by cool temperatures and may not be apparent in higher temperatures or after pod set; seed coat mottling brown or black depending on hilum color.
treatments: Late planting will reduce BPMV
Insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting management of the bean leaf beetle will reduce the incidence of BPMV
Early season management action (most damage is from early season introduction)
Managing weed hosts within fields may also reduce reservoirs for virus

summary.value: Green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy, with possible puckering and distortion of leaves and stunting of plants; pods may be misshapen with mottling and reduced size, and seeds may appear smoky or have seed coat mottling.
summary.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. In severe cases leaves may show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted. Pods can be misshapen with mottling and reduced in size. Infected plants may have green stems after the pods have matured. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling which will be brown or black depending on hilum color.

diagnostic_features.quote: Symptom development is favored by cool temperatures, but may not be apparent in higher temperatures or after pod set. Symptoms first appear on the younger leaves and can range from mild chlorotic mottling of foliage to a severe mosaic

look_alikes.quote: Higher yield losses can be observed if plants are infected on or before the V6 stage or co-infections with Soybean mosaic virus .

Insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting management of the bean leaf beetle will reduce the incidence of BPMV. Remember that most of the damage is from early season introduction, so trying to manage this disease during the pod filling stages will not have as much impact as an early season management action.

canonical.summary: CMV causes stunting, a mosaic pattern of light and dark green (or yellow and green) on leaves, malformation of leaves or growing points, yellow streaking or spotting, ring-spots or line patterns on leaves or fruit, flower color breaking, and yellowing of veins; in tomato, the most characteristic symptom is filiformity (thin, shoestring-like leaves).
diagnostic_features: The most characteristic symptom of CMV in tomato is filiformity, or the development of thin, shoestring-like leaves; in celery the most easily identified symptom is a downward curling of young petioles; in cucurbits prominent yellow foliar mosaic with downward leaf curl and greenish flower petals.
treatments: Plant resistant cultivars when available (resistant/tolerant cucurbit and pepper cultivars are available but there are no resistant tomato cultivars)
Purchase virus-free plants
Maintain strict aphid control
Mineral oil or rapid-acting insecticide sprays can be effective
Use reflective mulches (silver/polyethylene) to deter aphids and reduce virus transmission; place on planting beds before seeding or transplanting
Isolate fields with taller, non-susceptible crop borders (e.g. corn) to delay arrival of aphids into field and to act as depositories for virus
Time planting to avoid exposing young plants to high aphid populations (e.g. during aphid migrations)
Remove all weeds since these may harbor both CMV and aphids
Immediately set aside plants with suspect symptoms and obtain a diagnosis; discard virus-infected plants
Disinfest tools used for vegetative propagation frequently
Propagate plants via seed rather than vegetatively

summary.value: CMV causes stunting, a mosaic pattern of light and dark green (or yellow and green) on leaves, malformation of leaves or growing points, yellow streaking or spotting, ring-spots or line patterns on leaves or fruit, flower color breaking, and yellowing of veins; in tomato, the most characteristic symptom is filiformity (thin, shoestring-like leaves).
summary.quote: Symptoms associated with CMV infections: stunting mosaic pattern of light and dark green (or yellow and green) on the leaves. malformation of leaves or growing points yellow streaking of leaves (especially monocots) yellow spotting on leaves ring-spots or line patterns on leaves or fruit flower color breaking distinct yellowing only of veins

diagnostic_features.value: The most characteristic symptom of CMV in tomato is filiformity, or the development of thin, shoestring-like leaves; in celery the most easily identified symptom is a downward curling of young petioles; in cucurbits prominent yellow foliar mosaic with downward leaf curl and greenish flower petals.
diagnostic_features.quote: The most characteristic symptom of CMV in tomato is filiformity, or the development of thin, shoestring-like leaves.

look_alikes.quote: These symptoms can be confused with those caused by tobacco mosaic virus .

treatments.quote: There are no chemicals that cure a CMV-infected plant, nor any that protect plants from becoming infected. Purchase virus-free plants. Maintain strict aphid control. Remove all weeds since these may harbor both CMV and aphids. Immediately set aside plants with the above symptoms and obtain a diagnosis. Discard virus infected plants. Disinfest tools used for vegetative propagation frequently. Propagate plants via seed rather than vegetatively.

affected_parts.quote: Symptoms of CMV in cucurbits generally manifest as severe stunting, prominent yellow foliar mosaic, plant and leaf malformation, and stunting due to drastic reduction of leaf size and stem internodes.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cucumber mosaic virus in the cucumovirus group

type_of_disease.quote: CMV is made up of nucleic acid (ribonucleic acid, RNA) surrounded by a protein coat.

canonical.summary: Pale greenish-yellow spots (usually <1/4 inch, with no definite margins) form on the upper sides of leaves with olive-green to brown velvety mold on the lower leaf surface below the spots; leaves wither and die but often remain attached. Symptoms appear first on the lower leaves and progress upward.
diagnostic_features: Discrete yellow spots on the upper side of the leaf with corresponding olive-green spores/velvety mold on the underside; the oldest (lower) leaves are infected first and disease progresses from bottom to top, with white sporulation on leaf surface later in the disease progression.
treatments: Use drip irrigation and avoid watering foliage
Space plants to provide good air movement between rows and individual plants
Stake, string, or prune to increase airflow in and around the plant
Sterilize stakes, ties, trellises, etc. with 10% household bleach or commercial sanitizer
Circulate air in greenhouses or tunnels with vents and fans, and roll up high tunnel sides to reduce humidity
Keep night temperatures in greenhouses higher than outside temperatures to avoid dew formation on the foliage
Remove crop residue at the end of the season; burn it or bury it away from tomato production areas
Clean high tunnel or greenhouse walls and benches at end of season with a commercial sanitizer
Plant resistant varieties (Determinate: Red Mountain, Primo Red, Mountain Spring, Florida 97; Indeterminate: Geronimo, Trust, Boa; Heirloom: Cherokee Purple, Amish Paste, Prudens Purple)
Remove diseased plants and surrounding plants if symptoms are observed in the greenhouse
Dispose of infected plant material and sterilize equipment used in the greenhouse
Rotate crops and move tunnels regularly to prevent nutrient and disease pressure buildup
Use clean seed purchased from a reputable source
Apply fungicides prior to infection when environmental conditions favor disease
Alternate between different chemical families to avoid pathogen resistance
Organic options: Champ 30 WG / Champ (copper-based) and Zonix showed best control in organic trials
Conventional fungicides: Quadris Top (azoxystrobin + difenoconazole)
Conventional fungicides: Bravo Weather Stik (chlorothalonil)

diagnostic_features.quote: Discrete yellow spots on the upper side of the leaf and the corresponding olive spores on the underside. The oldest leaves are infected first.

treatments.quote: Plant resistant varieties Determinant varieties-A high tunnel trial at Cornell AgriTech showed the most resistant varieties to be Red Mountain and Primo Red. Mountain Spring and Florida 97 have also shown some resistance. Indeterminant varieties: Geronimo, Trust, Boa Heirloom varieties: Cherokee Purple, Amish Paste, Prudens Purple. Cultural practices If you observe symptoms in the greenhouse, remove the diseased plants and surrounding plants to prevent pathogen spread. Dispose of infected plant material and sterilize all equipment that was used in that greenhouse. Nutrients and disease pressures can build up in high tunnels, so rotate crops and move tunnels regularly. Make sure seed is clean and purchased from a reputable source. Applying chemical treatments In ongoing field studies, Champ 30 WG and Zonix showed the best control of tomato leaf mold in organic trials that took place in 2017 and 2018. Control methods for conventional growers include the Quadris Top product, containing azoxystrobin and difenoconazole or products such as Bravo Weather Stik, containing chlorothalonil.

affected_parts.quote: Foliage is often the only part of the plant directly infected. ... In severe cases, blossoms and fruit can also be infected, directly reducing yield. ... Fruit infections start as a smooth black irregular area on the stem end of the fruit.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Leaf mold is caused by the fungus Passalora fulva (previously called Fulvia fulva or Cladosporium fulvum ).

type_of_disease.quote: Leaf mold is caused by the fungus Passalora fulva

canonical.summary: Infected plants are often wilted, stunted, and may die prematurely; leaflets become small and turn brown, while the taproot and lower stem develop light gray streaking with small black specks (microsclerotia) beneath the epidermis giving a charcoal-sprinkled or marbled appearance, and reddish-brown discoloration develops in the pith and vascular tissues.
diagnostic_features: Presence of microsclerotia (small black specks) beneath the epidermis and inside the lower stem and taproot giving a charcoal-sprinkled or marbled appearance, with microsclerotia also in vascular tissue, pith, and mature dry pods; reddish-brown discoloration in pith and vascular tissues.
treatments: Manage fields to reduce or avoid drought stress, including irrigation when needed
Reduced tillage to keep soils cooler and reduce moisture stress
Crop rotation with non-host or low-susceptibility crops such as cereal grains
Reduced seeding rates to limit drought stress
Select cultivars with lower susceptibility (no fully resistant varieties available)
Fungicides are not typically recommended for managing charcoal rot

The taproot and lower stem may become streaked with light gray. Small black specks may form beneath the epidermis and inside the lower stem and taproot to give them a charcoal-sprinkled appearance.

diagnostic_features.quote: A characteristic sign of the pathogen on soybean is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, in the pith, and mature and dry pods. Cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot may become streaked with gray hyphae and microsclerotia, giving a marbled appearance

treatments.quote: Manage fields to reduce or avoid drought stress. Reduced tillage may reduce charcoal rot, perhaps due to cooler soils and less moisture stress. Rotation with crops that have relatively low susceptibility to charcoal rot, such as cereal grains, may be beneficial. Reduced seeding rates may also reduce drought stress and charcoal rot. Soybean cultivars may exhibit different levels of susceptibility, but none are fully resistant to this disease.

affected_parts.quote: At the seedling stage, typical symptoms include , brown to dark spots on cotyledons. At the unifoliate leaf stage (VC), circular to oblong, reddish-brown, lesions that eventually turn dark brown to black may be observed. Mid to late season aboveground symptoms include light brown to grey superficial lesions in the stem. A characteristic sign of the pathogen on soybean is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, in the pith, and mature and dry pods. Cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot may become streaked with gray hyphae and microsclerotia

canonical.summary: Gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown angular lesions (2-5 mm) develop first on the underside of leaves in the lower canopy, with small pustules (blisters) that break open and release masses of tan spores; lesions are associated with leaf chlorosis and may also occur on petioles, pods, and stems, with high lesion density causing defoliation and early maturity.
diagnostic_features: Angular lesions 2-5 mm in diameter on leaflets that become erumpant with small pustules (blisters) that break open and release masses of tan urediniospores; visible with a 20X hand lens, accompanied by leaf chlorosis.
look_alikes: other common leaf diseases on soybean
treatments: Scout the lower canopy of fields weekly, especially during wet weather after soybeans begin to flower
Soybean rust must be managed with the judicious use of fungicides applied properly and at the correct time
Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection
Use different modes of action if more than one fungicide is needed in a growing season (to manage fungicide resistance)
Fungicides are likely not needed if soybean rust is not within 100 miles of a field
Regularly scout fields throughout the growing season
Resistant soybean varieties are in development

summary.quote: A common symptom of soybean rust is the formation of gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown lesions that may become erumpant under the lesions on leaflets. Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems ( Figure 1 ). Lesions are associated with leaf chlorosis; high lesion density can cause defoliation and early maturity of the plant

look_alikes.value: other common leaf diseases on soybean

treatments.quote: The primary management of rust is through the use of fungicide applications. Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection for soybeans. Several fungicides are effective at reducing damages caused by rust, and efficacy can be found on the NC State Soybean Extension portal . Fungicide resistance is a concern with foliar pathogens, and different modes of action should be used if more than one fungicide is needed in a growing season. Fungicides are likely not needed if soybean rust is not within 100 miles of a field. ... Regularly scouting fields throughout the growing season is also important to monitor for foliar diseases like rust.

affected_parts.quote: Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems ( Figure 1 ).

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Asian soybean rust, caused by the fungus Phakopsora pachyrhizi, is a soybean pathogen described for the first time in the Southern portion of North Carolina in 2005.

type_of_disease.quote: The causal agent is the basidiomycete fungus, Phakopsora pachyrhizi.

canonical.summary: First symptoms on leaves are irregularly shaped, water-soaked lesions (light brown to tan) often with a lighter halo or pale green/gray border; in humid wet weather lesions appear greasy with white, fuzzy growth on the undersides of leaves and on lower stems; stem and petiole lesions are brown and ill-defined; tomato fruit appear mottled with firm, golden to dark brown, sunken (greasy) leathery surfaces.
diagnostic_features: White, fuzzy pathogen sporulation on the undersides of leaves, on lower stems, and around fruit lesions during high humidity; lesions are mostly on new growth and are light brown or tan (distinguishing from early blight, which starts on lower leaves with dark brown concentric rings); fruit are firm with greasy spots that become leathery and chocolate brown / golden to dark brown sunken surfaces.
treatments: Plant resistant cultivars when possible
Plant early in the season to escape high disease pressure later in the season
Eliminate volunteer tomato plants (re-seeded from the previous year's crop) and remove weeds
Space plants to increase airflow and reduce humidity
Do not allow water to remain on leaves for long periods
Scout plants often and remove infected plants and infected fruit
Sign up for alerts at the USAblight website
Apply preventive fungicides before initial infection; before disease occurs apply at 7-10 day intervals, after disease is detected in the area apply at 5-7 day intervals
Use Blitecast (disease-forecasting tool) to time preventive fungicide applications based on favorable weather
Alternate products and tank mix with chlorothalonil to avoid generating fungicide-resistant strains

Example fungicide products: Fluopicolide (Presidio 4FL); Oxathiapiprolin + chlorothalonil (Orondis Opti); Oxathiapiprolin + mandipropamid (Orondis Ultra); Propamocarb (Previcur Flex); Cyazofamid (Ranman 3.6SC); Ametoctradin + dimethomorph (Zampro); Mandipropamid + difenoconazole (Revus Top); Dimethomorph (Acrobat 50WP); Cymoxanil (Curzate 60DF); Mefenoxam + chlorothalonil (Ridomil Gold Bravo); Mancozeb + zoxamide (Gavel 75WG); Mancozeb (Dithane); Chlorothalonil (Bravo)
Fixed copper formulations (for organic growers)
Products containing copper or chlorothalonil (for home gardeners)

summary.quote: Infected leaves typically have green to brown patches of dead tissue surrounded by a pale green or gray border. When the weather is very humid and wet, late blight infections can appear water-soaked or dark brown in color, and are often described as appearing greasy. White, fuzzy growth may be found on the undersides of leaves or on lower stems. Stem and petiole lesions are brown and are typically not well defined in shape... Symptomatic tomato fruits appear mottled, often with golden to dark brown, firm, sunken surfaces.

diagnostic_features.value: White, fuzzy pathogen sporulation on the undersides of leaves, on lower stems, and around fruit lesions during high humidity; lesions are mostly on new growth and are light brown or tan (distinguishing from early blight, which starts on lower leaves with dark brown concentric rings); fruit are firm with greasy spots that become leathery and chocolate brown / golden to dark brown sunken surfaces.
diagnostic_features.quote: Lesions caused by late blight can be found anywhere on the plant, but are mostly found on the new growth whereas early blight typically starts on the lower leaves and slowly moves up the plant. Also, lesions caused by late blight tend to be light brown or tan in color while lesions caused by early blight tend to be dark brown in color with concentric rings.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Tomato late blight is caused by the oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans ( P. infestans ).

type_of_disease.quote: Phytophthora infestans is the oomycete, or water mold pathogen, responsible for tomato late blight.

canonical.summary: In cucurbits, root and crown rot appears suddenly as a permanent wilt with water-soaked, dark brown, soft roots and stems near the soil line and whole plants/vines collapsing quickly; in peppers, distinctive black lesions form on stems with wilted foliage and fruit remaining attached; fruit develop small water-soaked areas that enlarge and may be covered with yeast-like, gray to white sporangia.
diagnostic_features: In peppers, distinctive black lesions form on stems with stems remaining rigid while foliage and fruit wilt and wither but remain attached; sporangia-covered lesions have a yeast-like, gray to white appearance; on other hosts, the "powdered sugar" on infected plants is millions of lemon-shaped spores covering fruits, stems, crowns, or leaves in high humidity, with fruit developing soft spots and "melting."
treatments: Never dump culled fruit or plants into production fields
Know where your irrigation water comes from, and use un-infested water
Obtain compost from a trusted source
Promote good drainage and do not over-irrigate
Grow bushing cucurbits, tomatoes, peppers, and eggplants on raised beds
Plant tolerant/resistant varieties (e.g. pumpkins 'Lil' Ironsides', 'Apprentice', 'IronMan', 'Rockafellow', 'CannonBall'; bell peppers 'Conquest', 'Paladin', 'Emerald Isle')
Rotate with corn, small grains, brassicas, alliums, or other non-hosts (3-4 year minimum rotation)
Dispose of culled fruit or infected plants in a sanitary landfill, or by burying them
Prevent spread around your farm or into irrigation sources
Rogue infected plants and harvest early from an infected field
Keep records of contaminated fields; avoid planting susceptible crops in Phytophthora-infested soil
Separate susceptible crops so water cannot move from one planting to another
Do not plant low areas to susceptible crops
Wash soil from equipment after working in infested fields; always work in clean fields first
Break up hardpan and encourage drainage with sub-soiling tools
Avoid soil compaction; never work fields when wet
Plant non-vining cucurbits and peppers in dome-shaped raised beds of at least 9 inches
Use a transplanter that does not leave a depression around the base of the plant
Create breaks in raised beds and clear soil at row ends to prevent damming
Mulch between rows with straw or cover crop stubble to reduce splash dispersal
Biofumigation with brassica cover crops (especially mustards)
Practice good sanitation; do not discard cull fruit in the field
Disking localized infected areas may be worthwhile
mefenoxam/mancozeb (e.g. Ridomil, Apron)
copper compounds (e.g. Kocide, Basic Copper 53, Champ 2F)
phosphorous acid fungicides (e.g. Prophyt, K-phite, Agri-fos) as drench and foliar applications
biofungicides: Bacillus spp. (Serenade, Double Nickel, Taegro), Trichoderma spp. (Root Shield Plus), Streptomyces spp. (Actinovate, Mycostop)
Cucurbit fungicides: Presidio (FRAC 43), Ranman (FRAC 21), Zing! and Gavel (FRAC 22), Zampro and Revus (FRAC 40), Curzate or Tanos (FRAC 27)
Pepper additional materials: Omega 500 (FRAC 29), Reason 500 (FRAC 11) for suppression
Organic options: BioTam, Serenade Soil, Actinovate Ag
Apply fungicides labeled for buckeye fruit rot and late blight before storms with flooding potential to suppress foliar infection
Use chemical fungicides according to the label

summary.value: In cucurbits, root and crown rot appears suddenly as a permanent wilt with water-soaked, dark brown, soft roots and stems near the soil line and whole plants/vines collapsing quickly; in peppers, distinctive black lesions form on stems with wilted foliage and fruit remaining attached; fruit develop small water-soaked areas that enlarge and may be covered with yeast-like, gray to white sporangia.
summary.quote: In cucurbits, root and crown rot caused by P. capsici appears suddenly as a permanent wilt due to root infection following rain or irrigation. Roots and stems near the soil line will appear water-soaked, dark brown in color, and will become soft. Whole plants or vines collapse quickly. In peppers, P. capsici causes distinctive black lesions to form on stems. Infected stems remain rigid but foliage and fruit wilt and wither, and remain attached to the plant... Initially, symptoms will appear as small water-soaked areas that quickly enlarge and can become covered in sporangia in high humidity. Sporangia-covered lesions will have a yeast-like, gray to white appearance.

diagnostic_features.value: In peppers, distinctive black lesions form on stems with stems remaining rigid while foliage and fruit wilt and wither but remain attached; sporangia-covered lesions have a yeast-like, gray to white appearance; on other hosts, the "powdered sugar" on infected plants is millions of lemon-shaped spores covering fruits, stems, crowns, or leaves in high humidity, with fruit developing soft spots and "melting."
diagnostic_features.quote: In peppers, P. capsici causes distinctive black lesions to form on stems. Infected stems remain rigid but foliage and fruit wilt and wither, and remain attached to the plant... Sporangia-covered lesions will have a yeast-like, gray to white appearance.

look_alikes.quote: In tomato, P. capsici is one of the pathogens that can cause buckeye rot of tomato fruit.

canonical.summary: Causes a fruit spot and foliage blight: small (⅛ to ¼ inch) black, often angular spots on leaves (more prominent on undersides), which develop yellow halos as they age; on fruit, very small pinpoint-like dark brown to black spots that can be raised, flat or sunken and do not penetrate deeply.
diagnostic_features: On tomato leaves, bacterial speck produces small, angular (straight-edged) spots with yellow halos; on unripe green fruits, the spots often have darker green halos and on ripe fruits subtle yellow halos; fruit specks are very small (~1/16 inch) with surrounding tissue often more intensely green than unaffected areas.
treatments: Start with high quality, certified, pathogen-free seed or transplants from a reputable supplier
If seed is suspected to be contaminated, treat seed in hot water prior to planting to eliminate the pathogen
Produce disease-free transplants by raising them in an area where tomato production does not occur
Inspect all purchased transplants carefully; transplants originating in southern states should be certified
Do not use overhead sprinkler irrigation; use soaker or drip hoses to water at the base of plants
Only work with tomato plants when they are dry; avoid working in fields when bacterial diseases are present and fields are wet
Routinely disinfect garden tools with 10% bleach or 70% alcohol (rubbing alcohol)
Remove contaminated tomato debris at the end of the growing season; deep bury, burn (where allowed), or hot compost
Keep fields free from volunteers, weeds, and cull piles
Rotate fields/non-host crop rotation; do not replant tomatoes in the same area the following growing season

Apply copper-containing products labeled for tomatoes (copper/mancozeb combinations are most effective); first treatment must be applied before symptoms develop, with additional treatments every 10 to 14 days while cool, moist conditions continue

summary.value: Causes a fruit spot and foliage blight: small (⅛ to ¼ inch) black, often angular spots on leaves (more prominent on undersides), which develop yellow halos as they age; on fruit, very small pinpoint-like dark brown to black spots that can be raised, flat or sunken and do not penetrate deeply.
summary.quote: Leaf symptoms of bacterial speck consist of small black spots (approximately ⅛ to ¼ inch in diameter) that often are more prominent on the undersides of leaves.   As the spots age, a yellow halo may develop around the edge.  Spots on fruit are very small (almost pinpoint-like) and do not penetrate very deeply into the tissue.   The spots can be raised, flat or sunken, and range in color from brown to black.

diagnostic_features.value: On tomato leaves, bacterial speck produces small, angular (straight-edged) spots with yellow halos; on unripe green fruits, the spots often have darker green halos and on ripe fruits subtle yellow halos; fruit specks are very small (~1/16 inch) with surrounding tissue often more intensely green than unaffected areas.
diagnostic_features.quote: On tomato leaves, bacterial speck leads to small, angular (i.e., straight-edged) spots with yellow haloes.

look_alikes.value: Bacterial spot of tomato; Tomato spotted wilt
look_alikes.quote: Bacterial spot, (see UW Plant Disease Facts D0012,  Bacterial Spot of Tomato ) and tomato spotted wilt (a viral disease) may cause similar leaf symptoms.

treatments.quote: Start by using high quality, pathogen-free seed or transplants from a reputable seed supplier or garden center.  If you have seed that you believe is contaminated with the bacterial speck bacterium and would still like to use it (e.g., it's a favorite variety with difficult-to-find seed), consider treating the seed in hot water prior to planting to eliminate the pathogen.  ... DO NOT use a sprinkler to water; instead use a soaker or drip hose to water at the bases of plants.  Also, only work with tomato plants when they are dry, and consider routinely disinfecting garden tools with 10% bleach or (better due to its less corrosive properties) 70% alcohol (e.g., rubbing alcohol). ... use a product that is labeled for use on tomatoes and that contains copper as the active ingredient.  To be most effective, the first treatment must be applied before symptoms have developed.  Apply additional treatments every 10 to 14 days as long as cool, moist conditions continue.

affected_parts.quote: Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato causes a fruit spot and foliage blight.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bacterial speck of tomato is caused by the bacterium  Pseudomonas syringae  pv.  tomato .

type_of_disease.quote: Bacterial speck of tomato is caused by the bacterium  Pseudomonas syringae  pv.  tomato .

canonical.summary: Wilting of 1-2 leaves on young plants during the heat of the day, with recovery at night; as the disease progresses, leaves turn yellow and remain wilted, the area between veins browns, and the base of the plant may show brown cankers, root rot, and brown discoloration of the vascular tissue, eventually leading to permanent wilt and plant death.
diagnostic_features: A freshly cut stem at the base of the plant placed in clear water shows a stream of white slimy bacterial ooze (bacterial streaming), a strong indicator of bacteria in the vascular tissue; the interior of the stem is light to dark brown in the pith area; slimy tan-white to brownish ooze beads form where vascular tissue is cut.
treatments: Crop rotation (for at least 4 years) and planting cover crops of non-susceptible plants (i.e., corn, rye, beans, cabbage)
Weed control (weeds can serve as alternative hosts)
Use tomato plants grafted with bacterial wilt resistant rootstocks (e.g., Armada, Bowman, RST-05-113-TE, Shincheonggang, HNX12870564)
Remove infected plants immediately, placing them in a garbage bag or container to avoid spreading soil and bacteria
Grow and propagate from pathogen-free plant material; use pathogen-free potting soil or media
Establish stock plants tested and known to be free of the bacteria; train workers in sanitation procedures
Isolate all new, incoming plants even if certified as healthy; do not commingle with other plants
No chemicals or biological agents adequately control these bacteria

summary.value: Wilting of 1-2 leaves on young plants during the heat of the day, with recovery at night; as the disease progresses, leaves turn yellow and remain wilted, the area between veins browns, and the base of the plant may show brown cankers, root rot, and brown discoloration of the vascular tissue, eventually leading to permanent wilt and plant death.
summary.quote: Above-ground symptoms include wilting of 1-2 leaves on young plants during the heat of the day. Such plants tend to recover at night. ... Affected leaves turn yellow and remain wilted after a time. The area between leaf veins dies and browns. Usually, the main stem of the affected plants remains upright even though all the leaves may wilt and die. Internal symptoms include light tan to yellow-brown discoloration of the vascular tissue. Long sections of infected stems reveal dark brown to black streaking in the vascular tissue as the disease progresses.

diagnostic_features.value: A freshly cut stem at the base of the plant placed in clear water shows a stream of white slimy bacterial ooze (bacterial streaming), a strong indicator of bacteria in the vascular tissue; the interior of the stem is light to dark brown in the pith area; slimy tan-white to brownish ooze beads form where vascular tissue is cut.
diagnostic_features.quote: A freshly cut stem at the base of the plant placed in water can show a stream of a white slimy substance (fig. 2A) that is a strong indicator of the bacterium present in the vascular tissue (fig. 2B). The interior of the stem also can be a dark or light brown in the pith area (fig. 3).

look_alikes.value: Southern blight; Fusarium wilt; Verticillium wilt; Bacterial blight (Xanthomonas campestris pv. pelargonii)
look_alikes.quote: Bacterial wilt on tomato may be confused with other wilt or root rot diseases. Southern blight on tomato will also wilt, but affected plants will have a crown rot with white strands or mycelium growing up the stem from the soil line... Fusarium wilt of tomato will typically have one-sided leaf yellowing or wilting. Verticillium wilt typically causes V-shaped lesions on the leaflets and does not wilt as suddenly.

treatments.value: Crop rotation (for at least 4 years) and planting cover crops of non-susceptible plants (i.e., corn, rye, beans, cabbage); Weed control (weeds can serve as alternative hosts); Use tomato plants grafted with bacterial wilt resistant rootstocks (e.g., Armada, Bowman, RST-05-113-TE, Shincheonggang, HNX12870564); Remove infected plants immediately, placing them in a garbage bag or container to avoid spreading soil and bacteria; Grow and propagate from pathogen-free plant material; use pathogen-free potting soil or media; Establish stock plants tested and known to be free of the bacteria; train workers in sanitation procedures; Isolate all new, incoming plants even if certified as healthy; do not commingle with other plants; No chemicals or biological agents adequately control these bacteria
treatments.quote: Crop rotation and planting cover crops of non-susceptible plants (i.e. corn, rye, beans, cabbage) can reduce soilborne populations of the pathogen. If bacterial wilt is present in a field, remove infected plants immediately . Plants can be placed into a garbage bag or another container so as to not spread soil and bacteria to other plants... The use of bacterial wilt resistant rootstocks has been used successfully in fields where bacterial wilt is prevalent or widespread... Example rootstocks that are resistant to bacterial wilt include 'Armada', 'Shincheonggang', and 'HNX12870564'.

affected_parts.value: Root; Stem; Vascular; Foliar; Whole plant
affected_parts.quote: The bacteria can infect through roots and through any fresh wounds. ... Above-ground symptoms include wilting of 1-2 leaves on young plants during the heat of the day. ... Internal symptoms include light tan to yellow-brown discoloration of the vascular tissue. Long sections of infected stems reveal dark brown to black streaking in the vascular tissue as the disease progresses.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern bacterial wilt of tomato is caused by the soil-borne bacterium Ralstonia solanacearum (formerly known as Pseudomonas solanacearum ).

type_of_disease.quote: Bacteria called Ralstonia solanacearum attack almost 200 plant species in 33 different plant families.

canonical.summary: On tomatoes, uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions; on peppers, small brown necrotic spots and/or chlorotic ring spots form on foliage. Heavily infected leaves brown, roll, and wilt, infected seedlings show a "shepherd's crook" and die, and fruit shows yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas.
diagnostic_features: Bronzing of upper sides of young leaves with distinct necrotic spots, downward cupping of leaves, tip dieback, and chlorotic spots/blotches with concentric rings on ripe fruit; green fruit show slightly raised areas with faint concentric zones. Lesions caused by TSWV have blurred margins, while the margins of lesions caused by other diseases are definite.
treatments: Deploy TSWV resistant varieties (select varieties with the Sw-5 and Tsw genes)
Use virus- and thrips-free transplants from greenhouses that manage thrips and inspect transplants
Manage thrips on transplants before planting
Treating transplants with imidacloprid just before setting in the field
Use reflective mulch placed over plant beds prior to planting to disorient thrips or impair thrips feeding
Screen air intakes and use double-door entries to reduce TSWV by preventing entry of viruliferous vectors into greenhouses
Minimize thrips entry in greenhouses using thrips-proof screening (less than 169 microns, or mesh with no more than 100 holes per square inch)
Monitor thrips in the greenhouse with sticky yellow card traps
Avoid growing ornamentals and vegetables in the same greenhouse
Indicator plants as an early warning system for the presence of infective thrips in certain ornamental crops
Time planting to avoid major thrips migrations during critical early plant growth periods
Avoid planting near crops infected with Tomato spotted wilt virus
Monitor for thrips and tomato spotted wilt symptoms during the growing season
Consider removing infected plants at the seedling stage
Segregate externally sourced plant material; quarantine newly purchased plants; inspect transplants and discard symptomatic plants
Control weeds in and around fields (especially dandelions, sow thistle, chickweed, buttercup, and plantain)
Promptly remove and destroy old tomato plants and other host crops after harvest
Control weeds and volunteer plants in fallow fields, non-cropped, or idle land near next year's tomato field
Use biological control: minute pirate bugs (Orius insidious) and big-eyed bugs (Geocoris punctipes) as thrips predators
Spray tomatoes early in the season with neem oil or other botanical insecticides; summer oil or insecticidal soap may also be used
Use an integrated approach (variety, planting date, plant population, insecticide application, disease history, row pattern and tillage) as successfully applied in peanut in the Southeastern US
Develop and deploy transgenic plants that have virus-derived genes to confer resistance to tospovirus infection

Heavily infected leaves brown, roll, and wilt, infected seedlings show a "shepherd's crook" and die, and fruit shows yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas.
summary.quote: On tomatoes infected with TSWV, the uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions (Figure 1). Infected peppers form small brown necrotic spots and/or chlorotic ring spots on the foliage (Figure 2). Infected foliage on both tomatoes and peppers may become covered with so many spots that the entire leaf browns, rolls, and wilts. Infected seedlings form a "shepherd's crook" appearance and die (Figure 3)... Symptoms on the fruit of both tomatoes and peppers typically include yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas

diagnostic_features.value: Bronzing of upper sides of young leaves with distinct necrotic spots, downward cupping of leaves, tip dieback, and chlorotic spots/blotches with concentric rings on ripe fruit; green fruit show slightly raised areas with faint concentric zones. Lesions caused by TSWV have blurred margins, while the margins of lesions caused by other diseases are definite.
diagnostic_features.quote: Plants infected with Tomato spotted wilt virus exhibit bronzing of the upper sides of young leaves, which later develop distinct, necrotic spots . Leaves may be cupped downward. Some tip dieback may occur. On ripe fruit chlorotic spots and blotches appear, often with concentric rings. Green fruit show slightly raised areas with faint, concentric zones.

look_alikes.value: early blight; other leaf spot diseases; other plant viruses
look_alikes.quote: Symptoms of TSWV may also resemble symptoms of early blight or other leaf spot diseases... Keep in mind that some of these symptoms overlap with other plant viruses so it is important to get an accurate diagnosis for effective management.

treatments.quote: Deploy TSWV resistant varieties. When purchasing tomatoes and peppers, select varieties with the Sw-5 and Tsw genes, respectively... Treating transplants with imidacloprid just before setting in the field is one of the most effective approaches to suppress primary spread... Use reflective mulch... Control weeds. We specifically recommend controlling weeds such as dandelions, sow thistle, chickweed, buttercup, and plantain. Segregate externally sourced plant material... Use biological control methods... Minute pirate bugs (Orius insidious) and big-eyed bugs (Geocoris punctipes) are predators of thrips... Minimize thrips entry in greenhouses... thrips-proof screening on air intakes and vents... Holes in screening must be less than 169 micros, or mesh with no more than 100 holes per square inch... Monitor thrips in the greenhouse... Sticky yellow card traps... Avoid growing ornamentals and vegetables in the same greenhouse... Spraying tomatoes early in the season with neem oil or other botanical insecticides may help control thrips. Additionally, summer oil or insecticidal soap may be used.

affected_parts.quote: Chlorotic or necrotic rings form on the leaves of many infected hosts ( Figure 4 ), and may also appear on the fruits of some hosts ( Figure 1 ). Necrosis may develop in the foliage of some hosts... Although TSWV is not seed transmitted, it may cause the discoloration of seed produced on infected hosts... Stunting is a common symptom of TSWV infection

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: PATHOGEN:  Tomato spotted wilt virus

type_of_disease.quote: The  Tomato spotted wilt virus  (genus  Tospovirus ; family  Bunyaviridae ) (TSWV)

canonical.summary: Symptoms first appear as tiny, water-soaked areas that enlarge to circular or angular tan-to-gray lesions about 1/8 to 1/4 inch in diameter with dark margins and a grayish white center containing tiny black pycnidia, often surrounded by a yellow halo; heavily diseased leaves turn yellow, wither, and drop, starting at the base of the plant.
diagnostic_features: Circular tan-to-gray spots with darker brown margins, often surrounded by a yellow halo, dotted with dark, raised pycnidia (the fruiting body of the fungus) inside the lesion; develops on lower leaves first and progresses upward.
treatments: Rotate away from tomato (or susceptible solanaceous crops) for 2 or more years
Control presence of horsenettle and other host weeds
Remove crop debris from planting areas (particularly tomato crop debris)
Reduce the amount of time plants are wet (avoid over-irrigating)
Stake or trellis plants to improve air circulation and drying of leaves
Use mulches or plastic to reduce contact between leaves and soil
Avoid working in fields when leaves are wet
Scout plants often and remove infected plants/affected leaves
Clean and disinfect tools and equipment (stakes, cages) after leaving a field
Maintain proper spacing between plants
Plant resistant varieties
Remove 'suckers' or lowest lateral tomato plant growth
Use raised beds and rotate which bed has tomatoes from year to year
Apply clean tillage (e.g., plowing) to reduce primary inoculum

Apply recommended fungicides when conditions favor disease (azoxystrobin (Quadris); chlorothalonil (Bravo); chlorothalonil + cymoxanil (Ariston); difenoconazole + cyprodinil (Inspire Super); penthiopyrad (Fontelis); famoxadone + cymoxanil (Tanos); fluopyram + trifloxystrobin (Luna Sensation); fluxapyroxad + pyraclostrobin (Priaxor); fixed copper (Kocide); mancozeb (Penncozeb); mancozeb + fixed copper (ManKocide); mancozeb + zoxamide (Gavel); mandipropamid + difenoconazole (Revus Top); zinc dimethyldithiocarbamate (Ziram))
Organic options: fixed copper; potassium bicarbonate (Carb-O-Nator)

summary.value: Symptoms first appear as tiny, water-soaked areas that enlarge to circular or angular tan-to-gray lesions about 1/8 to 1/4 inch in diameter with dark margins and a grayish white center containing tiny black pycnidia, often surrounded by a yellow halo; heavily diseased leaves turn yellow, wither, and drop, starting at the base of the plant.
summary.quote: Symptoms first appear as tiny, water-soaked areas but soon enlarge to form circular or angular lesions about 1/8 to 1/4 inch in diameter. Mature lesions have a dark margin with a grayish white center that contains tiny, black fruiting (spore-producing) bodies. Heavily diseased leaves turn yellow, wither, and drop in large numbers, starting at the base of the plant.

diagnostic_features.value: Circular tan-to-gray spots with darker brown margins, often surrounded by a yellow halo, dotted with dark, raised pycnidia (the fruiting body of the fungus) inside the lesion; develops on lower leaves first and progresses upward.
diagnostic_features.quote: circular, tan-to-gray spots with darker brown margins and dotted with dark, raised pycnidia inside the lesion. These lesions are often surrounded by a yellow halo. The disease typically develops on lower leaves first, moving up to higher leaves as infection progresses.

canonical.summary: Occurs in wet, waterlogged, compacted soils with symptoms appearing during or shortly after waterlogged conditions. Plants may be stunted and chlorotic early in the disease cycle; infected stems appear bruised and soft, secondary roots are rotted, leaves turn yellow and brown, and plants can wilt and die. A characteristic dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem extends from below the soil line upward, with lesions continuous from the roots up the lower stem.
diagnostic_features: A dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem extends from below the soil line upward, with lesions continuous from the roots and up the lower stem. Wilted leaves remain attached to the plant after death. Plants are often killed in patches or in sections of rows.
treatments: Cultural practices that improve soil drainage to reduce saturated-soil conditions
Seed treatment or soil-applied fungicides containing mefenoxam (Apron XL) or metalaxyl (Apron) at the highest labeled rates
Plant resistant cultivars; the most widely effective resistance genes are Rps 1c and 1k, with Rps 1a, Rps 3, and other genes also effective in some areas
Where Rps genes are overcome by pathogen races, plant cultivars with high field tolerance (partial resistance)
Crop rotation and tillage may provide some benefit

treatments.quote: Try to create a field environment that reduces the likelihood of saturated soil by taking steps to increase drainage where possible. Treatment of seed with the highest labeled rates of fungicidal compounds such as mefenoxam (Apron XL®) or metalaxyl (Apron®) can be beneficial. In all areas where Phytophthora root and stem rot has been a problem, resistant cultivars should be planted. The most common resistance genes that are widely effective are Rps 1c and 1k. Rps1a can be effective, but is not as effective in many areas as the other genes. Rps 3 and other genes can also be effective. For areas where the Rps genes are not working well due to the presence of pathogen races that overcome the resistance, cultivars with high levels of field tolerance (or partial resistance) should be planted. Crop rotation and tillage may be of some benefit.

canonical.summary: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as tissue dies; spots darken and are surrounded by yellowish-green halos and often merge into large, dead patches, with dead tissue falling out to give leaves a ragged appearance.
diagnostic_features: Distinguished from Septoria brown spot by the presence of a yellow halo around lesions and by occurring on young leaves in the mid to upper canopy (whereas brown spot is usually seen on older, lower leaves); lacks fungal structures in lesions, and leaf veins may become affected creating necrotic areas resembling lightning on the leaf.
treatments: Plant resistant soybean varieties
Rotate with non-host crops
Always use pathogen-free seed
Avoid field cultivation when the foliage is wet
Deep tillage may help in the rare fields where this disease is frequently severe

canonical.summary: Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan; stems develop grayish to bleached white lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture, with hard black sclerotia later forming inside or outside stems and pods.
diagnostic_features: Grayish to bleached white stem lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture, and the production of hard, black, irregularly shaped sclerotia with a pink to white center inside or outside stems and pods; infected seeds may be flat and shriveled or replaced by sclerotia.
treatments: Select cultivars with high levels of partial resistance and an open canopy of upright leaves
Use long crop rotation with non-host crops such as corn and wheat to minimize inoculum build-up over time
Good weed management because several weeds are hosts for the pathogen
Tillage for fields with first occurrence of Sclerotinia stem rot to bury the sclerotia, while reduced tillage is preferred for fields with a long history of the disease
One year of moldboard plowing to bury sclerotia at least 10 centimeters in soil, delaying production of apothecia
Reduction of seeding rate to improve air circulation, reducing humidity and increasing temperature within the soybean canopy
Increasing row spacing to 30 inch rows to avoid complete canopy closure
Chemical control with fungicides (rotation of active ingredients); Boscalid has provided good efficacy in reducing disease incidence and severity
Lactofen herbicide to modify soybean canopy, delay flowering and induce systemic acquired resistance
Biological control with the fungus Coniothyrium minitans to reduce inoculum levels in the field
Avoid introduction of sclerotia in the field; clean seed-lots using spiral separators and gravity tables
Avoid harvesting heavily infested fields until end of season to prevent contamination of combines with sclerotia
Seed treatments with fungicides to treat infected seed
Where irrigation is used, reduce irrigation frequency during flowering
Low plant populations or wide rows may help reduce white mold severity

If the seeds were infected during the early stages of development, they may have a flat and shriveled appearance, or may be replaced by sclerotia.

treatments: Plant resistant varieties (numerous sources of resistance are available)
Crop rotation and burial/management of infested crop residue to reduce pathogen levels

treatments.quote: The disease rarely reduces yield, so fungicides are not recommended for downy mildew management. Implementing cultural controls to reduce the risk of downy mildew is generally all that is needed. Plant clean seed . Growers planting non-GMO varieties should use certified, disease-free seed. Do not plant seed from fields infected the previous year. Rotate crops and manage crop residue . The downy mildew fungus survives in crop residue and on the surface of seed. Longer crop rotations and placing crop residues into contact with the soil after harvest will help reduce future infections. Resistant varieties . Because downy mildew is not considered to be yield limiting, most seed companies do not provide resistance ratings. However, numerous sources of resistance are available.

canonical.summary: In high humidity, fluffy gray-brown velvety spore mass covers infected plant parts; leaves develop irregular to V-shaped tan to brown blotches starting at the leaf margin; stems show brown oval lesions that may girdle the stem in concentric rings causing wilting above the infection; flowers turn brown and die; infected fruit develop a pale, soft, watery (whitish) rot, with small 3-8 mm whitish rings (ghost spots) on the fruit surface from aborted infections.
diagnostic_features: Dense velvety gray-brown spore mass that releases a cloud of spores when disturbed; under a hand lens, spore-bearing structures resemble bunches of grapes; spores are light brown-gray on black stalks; stem lesions expand in concentric rings to girdle the stem; whitish soft rot on fruit with skin rupture near the center; small 3-8 mm whitish circular rings on fruit surface called 'ghost spots' from aborted infections.
treatments: No known B. cinerea resistant tomato cultivars are currently available
Apply fungicides preventatively prior to dense canopy growth; rotate fungicide groups and/or tank mix to manage resistance
Avoid overhead irrigation and minimize plant wetness; keep humidity and leaf wetness low by rolling up high tunnel sides and increasing ventilation
Do not work on wet plants; prune plants in the early afternoon (after morning dew has dried) so wounds dry quickly
Ventilate greenhouses to maintain relative humidity at less than 90%
Temperatures above 70°F discourage Botrytis development
Practice good greenhouse sanitation; scout, bag (in plastic), and remove infected stems, leaves, and fruit
Space plants well to avoid excess humidity in dense canopies (e.g., 20-24 inches between plants and 4-5 feet between rows)
Clean up leaf debris on the ground and remove all plant debris at end of season
Maintain consistent and adequate soil moisture; ensure adequate calcium for healthy tissue
Inspect transplants before planting and remove/destroy plants with severe Botrytis symptoms (active lesions, dead leaves or petioles)
Avoid unnecessary late irrigations; keep tops of beds dry when fruit is present
Cultural control is acceptable in an organically certified crop

Example fungicide products: Boscalid (Endura), Chlorothalonil + cymoxanil (Ariston), Difenoconazole + cyprodinil (Inspire Super 2.09SC), Fluxapyroxad + pyraclostrobin (Priaxor), Potassium phosphite + chlorothalonil (Catamaran), Dicloran (Botran), Chlorothalonil (Bravo)

The spores are light brown-gray on black stalks. Infected fruit develop a pale, soft, watery rot, or have white rings or halos on the fruit, called ghost spots.

summary.value: When fruit is infected, the area first becomes water-soaked, then soft and rotten, with rapidly growing abundant mycelium covering the affected part as clusters of fibrous gray sporangia; infected tissue eventually decomposes into water-like decay and may release juice with a fermented or acidic odor.
summary.quote: When the fruit is infected, the infection area is first soaked, and then becomes soft and rotten. The initial symptoms appear approximately one day after infection under appropriate temperature and humidity. Rapidly growing and abundant mycelium are observed on the surface of the fruit and cover the affected part by producing clusters of fibrous gray sporangia. The infected tissue is eventually decomposed into water-like decay, and its longitudinal section becomes very soft and slowly decays. And after about 2-3 days, the infected fruit may release juice with fermented or acidic odor

canonical.summary: Symptoms begin as water-soaked areas at stem axils or joints near the soil; the stem becomes covered with white cotton-like fluffy mycelium and is girdled, then becomes dry, discolored (bleached) and hard, and the plant wilts, collapses and dies.
diagnostic_features: Hard, grayish-black sclerotia about the size of a plump grain of rice develop, embedded in the stem; slicing the diseased stem longitudinally reveals these embedded structures, making positive identification fairly easy.
treatments: start with clean soil
frequently sanitize tools and other equipment in the high tunnel
rotating tomatoes with non-susceptible crops can reduce disease inoculum
For field plantings, rotating grasses like corn or small grain will help slow down the inoculum buildup
Deep plowing (e.g. at least 12 inches) can help to reduce disease severity by reducing the number of sclerotia at the surface of the soil
Strict sanitation including carefully removing and destroying diseased tissues and plants
maintaining a 'plant free zone' around the perimeter of the greenhouse
Pyraclostrobin (Cabrio) and fluxapyroxad + pyraclostrobin (Priaxor) are two materials labeled for the suppression of timber rot on tomato for field plantings only
the beneficial fungus Coniothyrium minitans (Contans) as a biocontrol agent that colonizes the soil and feeds on timber rot sclerotia

summary.quote: Symptoms of timber rot mostly begin as water-soaked areas from the stem axils or instem joints, either at or above the soil level... In time, the stem becomes covered with white "cotton-like" fluffy mycelium and girdled; later, the watersoaked area becomes dry, discolored (bleached appearance) and hard; and the plant (eventually) wilts, collapses, and dies.
